transcript.presenting_concern: Interpersonal effectiveness skills for conflict with roommate

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Hi Alyssa, come on in. How are you doing today? Before we start, did you bring your diary card?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah, I filled it out every day this time. I know I missed a couple days last week so I really tried to be consistent. Here.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Thank you. I appreciate the consistency — that's important data for both of us. Let me take a look. [reviewing card] So I'm looking at your emotion ratings across the week... I see some variation here, which is actually healthy. Not everything at a 9. But I do see you rated your urge to self-harm at a 6 on Tuesday and a 7 on Thursday. Those stand out. Tell me about those days.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Tuesday was the roommate thing. She left dishes in the sink again — like, a full pile, it had been there since Sunday — and I asked her to clean them. I tried to be calm about it but she said "you're not my mom" and literally walked out of the kitchen. Slammed her door. And I just... I went straight to my room and I wanted to cut so badly. I sat there with the box in my lap for like twenty minutes. Holding it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I want to pause here because you're telling me something really important. You had the box — your cutting supplies — in your lap. The urge was a 6. You sat with that for twenty minutes. But you didn't cut. What happened in those twenty minutes?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: At first it was just... I was flooded. I couldn't think clearly. I was so angry and hurt and the thought was like "she doesn't respect me, nobody respects me, I'm not even worth being polite to." And the anger was so big that the only thing that seemed like it would help was the pain. Like I needed to discharge it somehow.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the emotion was anger mixed with hurt, and the urge to cut was functioning as emotion regulation — a way to discharge an overwhelming feeling. That's the function we've identified before. What happened next?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I remembered you saying that I should wait ten minutes before acting on an urge. Like, just commit to ten minutes. So I looked at my phone clock and I said "okay, 6:43, I'll wait until 6:53." And during those ten minutes I used the ice thing we talked about. I went to the freezer — I had to walk past her closed door which was hard — grabbed ice cubes and brought them back to my room. I held them in my hands until it hurt enough to kind of... reset my brain. The cold was intense enough to give me that physical sensation without actually hurting myself.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And then what?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: By the time the ice melted, the 10 minutes were up and the urge had dropped from a 6 to maybe a 4. Still there but not screaming at me. Then I called my friend Megan. She didn't know what was going on specifically — I just told her I was having a bad night. She talked to me about her day, her dog, just normal stuff. By the time we got off the phone — maybe thirty minutes later — I was at like a 3. And I put the box away.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Alyssa, I want you to hear what just happened in the story you told me. You experienced a level 6 urge to self-harm. You were triggered by a rejection-sensitive interaction. You had the means right there. And you did NOT act on the urge. Instead, you used a delay strategy — the ten-minute commitment. You used ice as sensory grounding — a distress tolerance skill. You reached out for social support by calling Megan. And the urge came down from a 6 to a 3 naturally. That is incredible skill use under intense distress.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [quiet] When you list it out like that it sounds more impressive than it felt in the moment.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: In the moment it probably felt like white-knuckling it. Like barely surviving. But that IS the work. The skill use doesn't feel elegant — it feels desperate and clunky and hard. But it worked. You got through a high-urge moment without self-harm. How did it feel the next morning?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The next morning I felt... proud? Like genuinely. I woke up and my hands were a little sore from the ice but there were no new marks and I felt like I proved to myself I could handle it. That I'm not totally controlled by my emotions.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That pride is earned, Alyssa. I want you to hold onto it. That's evidence that you are building a new relationship with your emotions. They can be intense without being in charge. Now tell me about Thursday — the 7.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thursday was worse because it was about my ex. Jason. He posted a photo with this new girl on Instagram — like, clearly a couple photo. Arms around each other, some cute restaurant, her in his jacket. And I just spiraled. The thought was "he replaced me because I'm unlovable and broken. She's the normal version of a girlfriend. She doesn't have all my issues."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the trigger was seeing your ex with someone new, and the immediate interpretation was about your fundamental worthiness of love. Not "relationships end and people date other people" but "this proves something is wrong with me." And the urge was a 7?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Maybe even an 8 honestly. I was shaking. And crying. Ugly crying.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What did you do?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I... didn't do great at first. I went down the rabbit hole of looking at all her photos — this girl's entire Instagram. Comparing myself. She's prettier. She looks stable. She has photos of brunch with friends and hiking and a golden retriever. She looks like the kind of person who doesn't need a therapist and doesn't have scars on her arms. And I was like, of course he chose her. Anyone would choose her over me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: How long did that comparison behavior go on?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: About an hour. Maybe more. I lost track of time. I was just scrolling and hurting myself with it. It's like self-harm but with information instead of a blade.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's actually a really perceptive observation. The Instagram scrolling was functioning the same way cutting would — it was generating pain. It was giving you a sense of control over where the pain comes from. And it was probably reinforcing the belief that you deserve to hurt. Is that fair?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. Exactly. Like I was building a case against myself using her photos.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the comparing mind was fully activated. It was generating "evidence" that confirmed your worst belief about yourself. What happened after the hour?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I remembered the STOP skill. Not gracefully — it was more like I suddenly realized what I was doing and felt sick about it. I literally said "stop" out loud to myself. Like out loud in my room. Then the T — take a step back. I put my phone across the room on my dresser. The O — observe. I noticed I was hyperventilating a little, my face was hot, my chest hurt. And the P — proceed mindfully. I asked myself, "what do I actually need right now?" And the answer was... comfort. Physical comfort.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So you used STOP. And then what did you do with that awareness?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I ran a bath. As hot as I could handle. And I did the five senses grounding — I lit a candle, so there was the smell and the flickering light. Put on this playlist Megan made me of calm music, so hearing. The warm water on my skin, so touch. I focused on each one deliberately. By the time I got out — maybe forty minutes later — I was at a 4. Still felt terrible but not dangerous. Not a 7.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay. So I want to validate something here, and I also want to challenge something. The validation: even at a level 7-8, after an hour of rumination, you were eventually able to access your skills. You used STOP, five senses grounding, and self-soothing. The urge came down. You did not cut. That matters enormously. Now the challenge: that hour of comparison scrolling. You identified it as a form of self-harm. What would it look like to catch yourself earlier in that spiral next time?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I think... I need to not be on his Instagram at all. Because I know what I'm going to do. It's not like it's ever a neutral experience.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let's hold that thought — we'll come back to it. But I want to first acknowledge something about the timeline. A year ago, what would have happened when you saw that photo? At a 7 or 8 urge?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: A year ago I would have cut, immediately. And then I probably would have texted Jason something horrible. Either something rageful or something guilt-tripping. And then depending on how drunk I got, I might have driven to his apartment at 2 AM. I've done that before. More than once.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So compared to that pattern — self-harm, impulsive contact, potentially dangerous behavior while intoxicated — what happened Thursday, while painful, represents dramatically different choices. Even including the hour of scrolling. Can you see that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. Yeah, I can see it. It's just hard because it still FEELS like I'm barely coping. Like I'm one bad day from going back to the old patterns.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That fear of regression is really common at this stage of treatment. You've built new skills but they don't feel stable yet. They feel like a thin layer over the old habits. But think about it — you used skills twice this week at high intensity. The old patterns didn't even get close to happening. Your skills aren't a thin layer anymore. They're becoming your default, even if they don't feel automatic yet.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [small exhale] Okay. I'll try to remember that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let me note something else on your diary card. I see your sadness ratings were elevated most days — 5, 6, 7 range — but your joy had some moments too. Wednesday you rated joy at a 5. What happened Wednesday?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Oh — yeah. I went to the group therapy session and we did the mindfulness exercise. The one where you eat a raisin slowly? I know it sounds silly but I actually felt present during it. Like, for five minutes I wasn't in my head worrying about something. I was just there, tasting a raisin. And afterward I talked to Maya — she's in my group — and we went to get coffee. It was just... nice. Normal. I felt normal for an afternoon.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: "Normal" — what does that mean to you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Like... not being defined by my diagnosis. Not being the girl with BPD. Just being someone having coffee with a friend. Nobody needed to take care of me. I wasn't in crisis. We just talked about TV shows and her classes and it was easy.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That sounds like what we call in DBT "building a life worth living." Those moments of ordinary connection and ease — they're not trivial. They're the goal. The crisis management work we do is so you can have more Wednesdays and fewer Tuesdays and Thursdays.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I like that. More Wednesdays.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Now, let's talk about the interpersonal piece with your roommate, because I know that's been building for weeks. You mentioned Tuesday's interaction. What happened after that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: We didn't talk for two days. The apartment was so tense. Like, I could feel her avoiding me and I was avoiding her. We were doing that thing where you only come out of your room when you hear the other person in theirs. And I wanted to either scream at her or move out immediately. Classic me — all or nothing. Blow up the relationship or run from it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You're noticing the all-or-nothing pattern yourself now, which is great metacognition. The urge is: confront explosively or avoid completely. The middle path — which is what we've been working on — would be...?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Having a calm conversation where I express what I need without attacking her. DEAR MAN.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Did you end up doing that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Friday night. I was terrified. I spent like an hour rehearsing in my head. But I asked if she had a minute and if we could talk about the kitchen stuff. She said yes and sat down and I used DEAR MAN — well, I tried. Let me tell you what I said and you can tell me how I did.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I'd love that. Walk me through it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. D — describe the situation. I said "Hey, I've noticed that dishes have been sitting in the sink for a few days at a time. Like Sunday through Tuesday this week." I tried to keep it factual, no judgments. E — express how I feel. I said "When I come home and the kitchen is messy, I feel stressed and kind of overwhelmed. It affects my mood." A — assert what I want. I said "I'd like us to come up with a system — like maybe alternate days or a schedule — so we both know what's expected." R — reinforce. I said "I really value living with you and I don't want this to become a bigger issue. I think having clear expectations would make things better for both of us."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's excellent DEAR MAN. Really solid. What about the MAN part?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Mindful — I tried to stay on topic. She started to bring up how I left a wet towel on the couch once and I gently brought it back to the dishes without getting defensive. Appear confident — I made eye contact, sat up straight, didn't cry. Which was hard. Negotiate — that's where I was less sure. I asked what would work for her instead of just dictating.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And you said you also tried not to be like "do it or I'm leaving" — so you resisted the ultimatum. That's the middle path. What was her response?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: She was actually pretty receptive. She apologized for the "you're not my mom" comment — she said she knew it was harsh the second she said it. She said she'd been stressed about exams and took it out on me and that wasn't fair. We agreed to a cleaning schedule — alternate days for dishes, shared spaces get cleaned on Sundays together.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: How did that outcome compare to what you expected going into the conversation?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I expected her to get defensive. I expected her to tell me I'm too controlling or too sensitive or that I'm being dramatic. I expected it to turn into a fight. Maybe I expected her to say she wants to find a different roommate. My brain had like ten catastrophic versions planned.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And instead?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: She was reasonable. She was kind, actually. She said she appreciated that I came to her directly instead of being passive-aggressive about it. She said... she said "I didn't realize it was affecting you that much. I should have asked."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Sit with that for a moment. She appreciated you. She thanked you for how you handled it. That's the direct result of using your interpersonal effectiveness skills. When you don't attack, people don't defend. When you describe without judgment, people can hear you. When you express vulnerability — "this affects my mood" — people respond with empathy. You got your objective met AND preserved the relationship. That's DEAR MAN plus GIVE working together.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It felt really different from how I usually handle conflict. Usually I either stuff it down until I explode — like, weeks of resentment that comes out as this massive rage — or I go in guns blazing from minute one and burn the bridge. This was... neither of those.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And those two extremes — suppression or explosion — are the patterns we've been working to replace with middle-path skills. This week, you found the middle path. I want to highlight that this didn't happen by accident. You prepared. You rehearsed. You made a deliberate choice about how to approach the conversation. That's skillful means.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I guess I thought that eventually this stuff is supposed to be natural? Like I wouldn't need to rehearse for an hour?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Eventually, yes, it becomes more automatic. But even for people without BPD, difficult conversations take preparation. The difference is that right now you're learning a new language — interpersonal effectiveness is literally a new vocabulary for you. When you first learn a language, you translate in your head before speaking. Eventually you start thinking in it. You'll get there. For now, rehearsing is part of the process, not a failure.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That makes me feel better. I was worried I was being fake.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Preparing for a conversation isn't being fake. It's being intentional. The emotion behind it — your desire for a good living situation, your care about the relationship — that was completely genuine. You just chose a skillful delivery. That's wisdom, not fakeness.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. Yeah. [pause] Can we talk about the ex thing more? Because I feel like that's the bigger issue right now.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Absolutely. Let's go there. The Instagram situation — it touches on some of our longer-term work around self-worth and relationship patterns. You said the thought was "he replaced me because I'm unlovable and broken." Let me ask you — is that a thought you only experience when triggered by your ex, or does it show up in other contexts too?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: All the time. With everyone. Anytime someone gets close to me I'm waiting for them to leave once they see the real me. With friends — even Megan, I sometimes think she's going to get tired of me. With my family — my mom and I are better now but I still think she wishes she had a different daughter. With anyone I date. It's like this baseline belief that I'm fundamentally defective. That there's something wrong with me at my core that other people can see.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: In DBT we'd call that a vulnerability factor — it's part of your emotional baseline that makes you more susceptible to strong reactions when things happen. When something occurs that seems to confirm it — like your ex posting with someone new — the emotion is disproportionate to the situation because it's not just about the photo. It's hitting that core belief. The photo becomes "proof" of something you already feared was true.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It felt like proof. Like, there it is. He found someone better because I wasn't enough.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let me offer an alternative frame and you tell me how it lands. Jason broke up with you — correct me if I'm wrong — because he said the relationship was "too intense" and he felt like he was "walking on eggshells." Is that right?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. That's what he said. Which just confirmed the broken thing.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: But here's what I want you to consider. At that time — seven months ago — you were earlier in treatment. You were having more frequent crises. You had fewer skills. The relationship difficulties weren't because of some permanent flaw in you — they were because of untreated or under-treated BPD symptoms. The same skills you used THIS week — DEAR MAN with your roommate, STOP when triggered by the photo, distress tolerance with the ice — those would have changed the dynamic with Jason. You weren't incapable of a healthy relationship. You hadn't yet learned the skills for one.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] I never thought about it that way. I always just thought... I was too much. Inherently.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: "Too much" is a judgment, not a fact. The DBT frame is different: you had emotional vulnerability and you hadn't yet developed effective regulation and interpersonal skills. That's a skills deficit, not a character deficit. And skills can be learned — as you're demonstrating every single week.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: But what if the next person I date also can't handle me? Even with skills?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a "what if" — future-focused worry about something that hasn't happened yet. Let's stay present. Right now, in this moment, what's the evidence from THIS week about your ability to be in relationship with others? Not romantic relationship, but relationship broadly?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I mean... I handled the roommate situation well. I called Megan and she showed up for me. I went to group and connected with Maya. I'm here.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Right. You navigated conflict, maintained a friendship, built a new connection, and engaged in treatment. Those are relational competencies. They're happening right now, not in some hypothetical future. The evidence from this week says you can be in relationship with people. Not perfectly — but effectively.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So what do I do about the "unlovable" feeling? Because it's there even when the evidence contradicts it. Like right now I can hear what you're saying and I believe it intellectually but the feeling in my chest is still saying "but you ARE broken."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Here's what I'd invite you to notice. That belief — "I am broken, I am unlovable" — what's the emotion attached to it right now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Sadness. Deep sadness. Like grief. Like I'm mourning something.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What are you mourning?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [starting to cry] I think... I'm mourning the version of me that could have existed if I didn't have this disorder. Like, the normal Alyssa. The one who wouldn't have driven away Jason. The one who doesn't need to rehearse for an hour before a basic conversation. The one who doesn't sit with a box in her lap at 22 years old.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [gentle pause] That grief is valid, Alyssa. I want you to hear that. You have every right to grieve the ease that other people seem to have. It IS harder for you. That's not fair. And it's also not your fault. BPD develops from a combination of biological temperament and environmental invalidation. You didn't choose this.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [crying] No, I didn't. I really didn't.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And here's the dialectic — and I know you know this word by now — the both/and. You can grieve the difficulty AND recognize the growth. Both are true. "This is harder for me than for most people AND I am learning to do it anyway." "I have BPD AND I am building a life worth living." "I feel unlovable AND people in my life consistently show me they care."

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [wiping eyes] Both/and instead of either/or.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. The either/or thinking — "I'm either completely fine or completely broken" — that's the black-and-white pattern of BPD itself. Reality lives in the middle. You are a person with struggles AND strengths. A person who is still learning AND already capable.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [deep breath] Okay. Okay. I needed to hear that today. I think I've been in the "broken" story all week and nobody challenged it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I'm glad you let me challenge it here. That's what this space is for. Now, I want to give you some homework that's specifically designed to target this belief. For this week, I'd like you to continue your diary card — that's non-negotiable, every day. But I'd also like you to practice the opposite-action skill specifically around the "unlovable" belief. Do you remember how opposite action works?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. When the emotion doesn't fit the facts or isn't effective, you do the opposite of what the emotion is urging you to do.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Right. So when the "unlovable" thought shows up, the urge is typically to...?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Isolate. Hide. Cancel plans. Withdraw.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the opposite action would be...?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Reach out. Connect. Say yes to something instead of no.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. When you notice that thought this week — "I'm unlovable, I'm broken" — I want you to do one thing that reflects connection. Text a friend. Accept an invitation. Call your mom. Reach out to Maya from group. Something that says "I am a person who belongs in relationships" even when you don't feel it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That's going to be hard. When I feel that way, all I want to do is disappear. Like, physically make myself small and invisible.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I know. And the hiding maintains the belief. If you never test it — if you always withdraw when you feel unlovable — you never get evidence that people want you around. You're living inside the belief without ever checking it against reality. The opposite action breaks that cycle. Start small — even a single text counts. Even an emoji reply counts.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. I can do texts. I can start there. I'll try.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's all I'm asking. Try. And track it on your diary card — note when you used opposite action and what happened.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Now — the Instagram. You said earlier that you think you need to not be on his Instagram at all. Let's talk about that. What would you think about unfollowing your ex? Not as an act of anger or revenge, but as an act of self-care. Reducing exposure to a known trigger.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I've been thinking about that a lot. Part of me feels like if I unfollow, I'm being dramatic. Or weak. Or like I'm not over him. Like people would judge me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Who specifically would judge you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I don't know... him? If he notices? He'd think I'm still hung up on him.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And if he thinks that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] ...Does it matter? He's not in my life anymore.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Wise mind is talking right now. Keep going.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. So... why am I keeping a window open into someone's life that only makes me feel terrible? Every time I see a post, one of two things happens: either I feel worthless because he's happy, or I feel satisfied because he seems unhappy and then I feel guilty for being a bad person. There's no good outcome from checking.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So emotion mind says "don't unfollow, you'll seem dramatic." Rational mind says "it's clearly harmful and serves no purpose." What does wise mind say?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Wise mind says unfollow. And probably mute too so I can't look him up when I'm upset.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: When you're ready. No pressure for a timeline. But I think that's a wise-mind decision. And I want to name something — the ability to identify what wise mind is saying, even when emotion mind is loud, is a skill you've developed. Fourteen sessions ago you couldn't distinguish between those voices.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [small smile] Yeah. I remember session one when you explained wise mind and I was like "I don't have that. I just have screaming mind and numb mind."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [laughs] I remember that too. And look at you now — accessing wise mind in real time during session. It's there, Alyssa. It's been getting stronger every week.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Can I ask something kind of random?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Of course.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My mom wants to have dinner this weekend. Just us. And usually those dinners go one of two ways — either we're fine and it's nice, or she says something critical and I lose it. Last time she made a comment about my weight and I didn't talk to her for a week. I want this one to go well. Can we... can we prep for it?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Absolutely. Let's think about this using GIVE — since with your mom, the priority is usually relationship maintenance rather than getting a specific objective. What's the relationship goal for dinner?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I want to have a nice evening. I want to feel connected to her. And if she says something critical, I want to be able to not blow up or shut down.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay. So GIVE: be Gentle — no attacks, no threats, no judgments even if she says something that stings. Act Interested — ask about her life, listen. Validate — acknowledge her perspective even if you disagree. Easy manner — keep it light, use humor if appropriate. Does that framework feel applicable?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. The hardest one will be the "gentle" if she criticizes me. Because my automatic response is to either get really nasty back or to go completely silent and cold.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What if we planned a response for that specific scenario? If she says something critical — about your weight, your life choices, your apartment, whatever — what could you say that's gentle but also honest?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Maybe... "Mom, that comment hurts my feelings. Can we talk about something else?" Simple. Direct. Not mean.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's beautiful. It's assertive without being aggressive. It names the impact — "that hurts" — without attacking her character. And it offers a redirect. If she responds well to that, great. If she pushes, you could add "I love spending time with you and I don't want to argue tonight."

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. Yeah, that feels doable. Having the words ready helps because in the moment my brain goes blank and I either explode or freeze.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Rehearsal reduces the chance of falling into those automatic patterns. You're creating a plan for wise mind to follow when emotion mind tries to take over. Good. So your homework for this week: diary card every day, opposite action when the "unlovable" belief shows up, practice GIVE at dinner with mom. Anything else before we close?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I don't think so. This was a good session. I actually feel like I'm making progress for the first time in a while. Like, real progress. Not just surviving-between-crises progress.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You ARE making progress. And I want you to notice how you just said that — "I feel like I'm making progress" — without immediately taking it back or qualifying it. That's new too.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [laughs] You're right. Usually I'd follow that with "but probably not really" or "watch me relapse next week."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: But you didn't. You let the positive statement stand. That's growth in itself. The data on your diary card shows it — fewer high-urge days compared to two months ago, more skill use, successful interpersonal interactions, moments of genuine joy. This is real, measurable change, Alyssa. And you did it. Not me — you. You're the one using the skills in your real life when it's hard.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [quiet] Thank you for saying that. I needed it today.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Of course. I'll see you next Tuesday. Go enjoy Wednesday energy this week.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [smiling] More Wednesdays. Got it. See you Tuesday. Thanks, Dr. Torres.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Take care, Alyssa.

goals: Interpersonal effectiveness skills for conflict with roommate
